For a demonstration of a line plot on a polar axis, see Figure 1.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.arange(0, 2, 0.01)
theta = 2 * np.pi * r
fig, ax = plt.subplots(
  subplot_kw = {'projection': 'polar'} 
)
ax.plot(theta, r)
ax.set_rticks([0.5, 1, 1.5, 2])
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()

title: Projects
text:
Developed a pipeline for automated meshing and simulation of stented coronary arteries. This methodology has been applied to several patient-specific cases. Due to copyright of patient data, the demonstration of this pipeline is constrained to synthetic STL geometries that closely resemble real patient-specific geometries. This was done with all open-source software on a low-end Mac Mini M4 with 16 GB RAM, 10 CPU cores, 10 GPU cores ($500 dollar price-tag). Complete meshing setup, tagging, smoothing, and domain decomposition was done without human intervention in under 3 minutes. All visualization presented here was done with ParaView, an open-source visualization software.

title: Projects
text:
Nanopore embedding in microscale simulations (2025)
Worked on developing feasible algorithms for numerical simulations of micromaterials with embedded nanopores in three-dimensional space without sacrificing time and spatial performance. I accomplished this through existing meshing strategies consisting of adaptive refinement and coarsening, our mesh approximation algorithm, and bounding volume hierarchy (BVH) algorithms. 
Digitally recreated foam from experiments.

title: Projects
text:
Geometrical approximation of meshes (2025)
Constructed meshing reassignment algorithms to approximate complex porous regions and geometries. Achieved the following computational feats:

Approximated geometries from without changing initial STL file



Built systems architecture to automatically generate and run more than 850 simulations through clusters via HPC and cross-coding among Python, MATLAB, and COMSOL.



Established computational validation and simulation equivalence between actual geometries and their counterpart mesh approximations.



Beat benchmark simulation running time (219 seconds versus 18050 seconds) and RAM usage (24 GB versus 318 GB)

title: Projects
text:
Pore-detection Algorithms (2024)
Developed pore-detection algorithms that prove superior against watershed methods. Obtained the following results:

More precise pore-centers, especially by eliminating instances of pore intersections
Pore center estimates of radius 50 pixels after using our pore intersection method (left) versus watershed (right). 


Bridged non-visible pore borders.

title: Projects
section: Modeling and simulation of coronary stents (2023)
text:
Modeling and simulation of coronary stents (2023)
In this project, we developed multi-domain modeling of coronary stents taking into account the full interplay among the wall, lumen, and struts through fluid-structure interactions. 












Paper (ACSE 2024) 
Stent Modeling

title: Irving Martínez Acosta
text:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Number of Fourier terms
n_terms = 8  # You can adjust this value to increase accuracy

# Define the time parameter t (from 0 to 2*pi)
num_points = 50  # Number of points for plotting and DFT
t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, num_points)

# Define the exact parametric heart shape
x_exact = 16 * np.sin(t)**3
y_exact = 13 * np.cos(t) - 5 * np.cos(2*t) - 2 * np.cos(3*t) - np.cos(4*t)

# Perform a Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) to get the Fourier coefficients
def compute_fourier_coefficients(signal, n_terms):
    # Compute the DFT of the signal using numpy's FFT
    fft_coeffs = np.fft.fft(signal) / len(signal)
    
    # We only need the first n_terms/2 positive frequencies (real and imaginary parts)
    # We take real and imaginary parts to construct cosine and sine terms
    a_coeffs = 2 * fft_coeffs.real[:n_terms//2]
    b_coeffs = -2 * fft_coeffs.imag[:n_terms//2]
   
    # DC term (n=0) should not be doubled
    a_coeffs[0] = fft_coeffs.real[0]
    b_coeffs[0] = 0.0  # DC term for sine is always zero

    return a_coeffs, b_coeffs

# Compute Fourier coefficients for x(t) and y(t)
A_x, B_x = compute_fourier_coefficients(x_exact, n_terms)
A_y, B_y = compute_fourier_coefficients(y_exact, n_terms)

# Reconstruct the Fourier series approximation of the heart shape
x_approx = np.zeros_like(t)
y_approx = np.zeros_like(t)

# Add the Fourier series terms up to n_terms
for n in range(n_terms // 2):
    if n == 0:
        # DC term (n=0)
        x_approx += A_x[n]
        y_approx += A_y[n]
    else:
        # For n > 0, add cosine and sine terms
        x_approx += A_x[n] * np.cos(n * t) + B_x[n] * np.sin(n * t)
        y_approx += A_y[n] * np.cos(n * t) + B_y[n] * np.sin(n * t)

# Plot the exact heart shape and the Fourier series approximation
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
plt.plot(x_exact, y_exact, 'k--', label='Exact Heart Shape', alpha=0.5)
plt.plot(x_approx, y_approx, 'r', label=f'Fourier Series Approx. (n={n_terms})', linewidth=2)
plt.title(f'Fourier Series Approximation of a Heart (n={n_terms})')
plt.xlabel('x(t)')
plt.ylabel('y(t)')
plt.legend()
plt.axis('equal')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

title: Irving Martínez Acosta
text:
About
I am a mathematical scientist working on applied mathematics, scientific computing, and modeling. My interests include numerical analysis, PDEs, inverse problems, and data‑driven methods.
I focus on bridging mathematical theory with computational practice, with applications in physics and engineering.

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text: This document provides a comprehensive derivation and simulation of incompressible duct flow. We explore the transition from inviscid potential theory to the viscous Navier-Stokes equations, focusing on the mechanics of acceleration and pressure recovery.

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text: This document provides a comprehensive derivation and simulation of incompressible duct flow. We explore the transition from inviscid potential theory to the viscous Navier-Stokes equations, focusing on the mechanics of acceleration and pressure recovery.

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text:
§1 The Geometry
The duct follows a contraction–throat–diffuser profile defined by a smooth cosine contraction and a linear expansion.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp

def duct_h(x, h_in=0.36, h_t=0.14, angle_deg=8.0):
    x_c, x_t = 0.35, 0.45
    alpha = np.tan(np.radians(angle_deg))
    x = np.atleast_1d(x)
    h = np.empty_like(x)
    h[x < x_c] = h_in + (h_t - h_in) * 0.5 * (1 - np.cos(np.pi * x[x < x_c] / x_c))
    h[(x >= x_c) & (x < x_t)] = h_t
    h[x >= x_t] = h_t + alpha * (x[x >= x_t] - x_t)
    return h

x_arr = np.linspace(0, 1, 500)
h_arr = duct_h(x_arr)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3), facecolor='#0f1117')
ax = plt.gca()
ax.set_facecolor('#141820')
ax.fill_between(x_arr, -h_arr, h_arr, color='#4a9eff', alpha=0.2)
ax.plot(x_arr, h_arr, color='#8ab0d0', lw=2)
ax.plot(x_arr, -h_arr, color='#8ab0d0', lw=2)
plt.title("Duct Geometry", color='white')
plt.show()











Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from matplotlib.collections import LineCollection
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# ── Geometry ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def duct_half_height(x, h_in=0.36, h_throat=0.14, angle_deg=8.0):
    """Half-height h(x) for x in [0,1]."""
    x_contract_end = 0.35
    x_throat_end   = 0.45
    alpha = np.tan(np.radians(angle_deg))
    h = np.empty_like(np.atleast_1d(x), dtype=float)
    for i, xi in enumerate(np.atleast_1d(x)):
        if xi < x_contract_end:
            s = xi / x_contract_end
            h[i] = h_in + (h_throat - h_in) * 0.5 * (1 - np.cos(np.pi * s))
        elif xi < x_throat_end:
            h[i] = h_throat
        else:
            h[i] = h_throat + alpha * (xi - x_throat_end)
    return h if h.ndim > 0 else h[0]

x_arr = np.linspace(0, 1, 400)
h_arr = duct_half_height(x_arr)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 3))
ax.fill_between(x_arr, h_arr, 0.5, color='#1a2a3a', alpha=0.9)
ax.fill_between(x_arr, -h_arr, -0.5, color='#1a2a3a', alpha=0.9)
ax.plot(x_arr,  h_arr, 'steelblue', lw=2)
ax.plot(x_arr, -h_arr, 'steelblue', lw=2)
for xt, lbl in [(0.17,'Contraction'),(0.40,'Throat'),(0.72,'Diffuser')]:
    ax.text(xt, -0.48, lbl, ha='center', fontsize=9, color='#8ab0d0', style='italic')
ax.set_xlim(0,1); ax.set_ylim(-0.52,0.52)
ax.set_facecolor('#0f1117'); fig.patch.set_facecolor('#0f1117')
ax.tick_params(colors='gray'); ax.set_xlabel('x (normalized)', color='gray')
ax.set_ylabel('y', color='gray')
ax.set_title('Duct geometry: contraction → throat → diffuser', color='#c8d8f0')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text:
§2 Potential Flow and Bernoulli
In inviscid flow, the speed \bar{U}(x) is inversely proportional to h(x). Bernoulli’s principle states 
p + \frac{1}{2}\rho U^2 = \text{const}.



Code
Q = 0.08
rho = 1.0
U_bar = Q / (2 * h_arr)
p_arr = 1.0 + 0.5 * rho * (U_bar[0]**2 - U_bar**2)

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=x_arr, y=U_bar, name='Velocity Ū'))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=x_arr, y=p_arr, name='Pressure p'))
fig.update_layout(template='plotly_dark', title="Quasi-1D Potential Flow")
fig.show()

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text:
§3 The Navier–Stokes Equations
We consider steady, incompressible flow:  ho(\mathbf{u}\cdot
abla)\mathbf{u} = -
abla p + \mu
abla^2\mathbf{u} 
In the contraction, convection dominates; in the diffuser, the adverse pressure gradient and viscosity can lead to separation.

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text:
§4 Viscosity and the Boundary Layer
Viscosity causes the no-slip condition at the wall, creating parabolic velocity profiles.


Code
def profile(y, h, U_avg):
    return 1.5 * U_avg * (1 - (y/h)**2)

h_throat = duct_h(0.4)[0]
U_throat = Q / (2 * h_throat)
y_vals = np.linspace(-h_throat, h_throat, 100)
u_vals = profile(y_vals, h_throat, U_throat)

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.plot(u_vals, y_vals)
plt.title("Viscous Velocity Profile at Throat")
plt.show()

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text:
§5 Particle Trajectories (Animated)
We integrate the Lagrangian paths \frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{u}.


Code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp

def get_vel(x, y, Q=0.08, mode='viscous'):
    h = duct_h(x)[0]
    u_avg = Q / (2*h)
    u = 1.5 * u_avg * (1 - (y/h)**2) if mode=='viscous' else u_avg
    # Approximation for v via continuity
    dx = 1e-4
    dh_dx = (duct_h(x+dx)[0] - h) / dx
    v = -y * (u_avg/h) * dh_dx
    return u, v

n_particles = 9
y0s = np.linspace(-0.25, 0.25, n_particles)
t_eval = np.linspace(0, 2.5, 120)

frames = []
particle_data = [solve_ivp(lambda t, p: get_vel(p[0], p[1]), [0, 2.5], [0, y], t_eval=t_eval).y for y in y0s]

for k in range(len(t_eval)):
    frame_pts = []
    for p in particle_data:
        frame_pts.append(go.Scatter(x=[p[0, k]], y=[p[1, k]], mode='markers', marker=dict(size=6)))
    frames.append(go.Frame(data=frame_pts, name=str(k)))

fig_anim = go.Figure(data=frames[0].data, frames=frames)
fig_anim.update_layout(xaxis=dict(range=[0, 1]), yaxis=dict(range=[-0.5, 0.5]),
                       updatemenus=[dict(type="buttons", buttons=[dict(label="Play", method="animate", args=[None])])],
                       template='plotly_dark')
fig_anim.show()

title: Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct
text:
§6 Final Summary Dashboard
A unified view of the duct’s performance fields.


Code
fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 8), sharex=True)
axs[0].plot(x_arr, U_bar, color='lime', label='Velocity')
axs[0].set_ylabel("m/s")
axs[1].plot(x_arr, p_arr, color='orange', label='Pressure')
axs[1].set_ylabel("Pa")
plt.show()
